EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down along Highway 62 and Lower River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large shield of precipitation spread across southeast Georgia as an area of low pressure developed off the Mid Atlantic coast. Increasingly cold air began to fill in behind a cold front, setting the stage for rain to gradually change over to snow or a mix of rain and snow. Light snow accumulations occurred across a few interior southeast Georgia counties, though there were no significant impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor snow accumulation ranging from a dusting to a two tenths of an inch were reported at several locations across the county. This includes the intersection of Highway 21 and High 25 just north of Millen, Highway 25 southwest of Scarboro, along Highway 17 between Millen and Scarboro, and near the Screven and Jenkins county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass overspread the southern Appalachians in the wake of a strong cold front throughout the 26th and 27th. Widespread air temperatures in the single digits combined with gusty northwest winds to produce wind chill values of 0 to 10 below.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving wave of low pressure developing along a stationary front draped across the region resulted in periods of heavy rain across the northeast Georgia mountains beginning on the morning of the 9th and continuing off and on for over 24 hours. A swath of 4 to 5.5 inches of rain fell across central and southern Rabun County, resulting in some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported flash flooding developed across portions of central and southern Rabun County after 4 to 5.5 inches of rain fell across the area in around 24 hours, with around half of that falling in around six hours during the morning of the 10th. Impacts began with an embankment failure along Highway 76 just west of Clayton, where a lane of the highway was covered and a vehicle briefly trapped. Stekoa Creek overflowed its banks just south of Clayton, with water inundating Rickman Airfield Rd. Chechero Creek also  overflowed onto Country Club Dr near Highway 76. Additionally, minor flooding was reported in spots along Tiger Creek from the headwaters to its confluence with the Tallulah River. Portions of Old Highway 441 were inundated, including at Shady Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted much of southeast Georgia with significant snow accumulations and very cold wind chill temperatures. An area of low pressure shifted off the Southeast coast while deepening, ushering in a very cold air mass across the region. The depth of cold air was sufficient to support snow as the primary precipitation type. Rain quickly transitioned to snow during the afternoon hours, which continued into the evening before ending. Most of the snow accumulation impacted areas along and north of Interstate 16, with the highest amounts reported across Screven, Bulloch, and Effingham counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local criteria for an Extreme Cold Warning is temperature or wind chill of 10 degrees or less. The wind chill temperature first fell below 10 degrees at the National Ocean Service tide gauge at Fort Pulaski at 21:48 EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Severe to Extreme Drought that characterized Fall 2025 persisted into January 2026 as north and central Georgia failed to benefit from the typical 'recharge' period. A more active weather pattern in the second half of the month allowed for several rounds of beneficial, albeit non-drought-breaking, rainfall, particularly over the north and northwest portions of the state. While these systems helped stabilize soil moisture in some areas, deep-layer moisture deficits remained significant, and led to the redevelopment of D3 Extreme Drought over portions of the state.||Precipitation for January varied across north and central Georgia. Much of north Georgia and the Metro Atlanta area received between 3.0 and 5.0 inches of rain, which neared 60 to 100 percent of the monthly normal. In contrast, central Georgia continued to lag behind, with many areas receiving only 1.5 to 3.0 inches (approximately 40 to 60 percent of normal). Despite the uptick in surface moisture, the cumulative rainfall deficit since August 2025 remained between 8 and 14 inches for a large swath of the Piedmont. Consequently, while topsoil showed signs of recovery, subsoil moisture and groundwater levels remained low for mid-winter.||Agricultural impacts continued to be reported through the Condition Monitoring Observer Reports (CMOR). The winter wheat crop, already stunted by the dry fall months, showed uneven growth and poor tillering in the most affected central Georgia counties. Livestock producers remained in a critical state; although January rains provided some minor recharge to stock ponds, many remained at dangerously low levels. The reliance on supplemental hay feeding remained total across central Georgia, and the high cost of feed continued to drive reports of decreasing herd size as producers looked toward a potentially dry spring.||The U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM) reflected a month of slow expansion of drying conditions. At the start of January, roughly 15.4 percent of north and central Georgia was in D2 Severe Drought. By the end of the month, this figure had shifted slightly to 50.96 percent. Although there were some improvements over portions of the western and northern greater Metro Atlanta area, there were also pockets of D3 Extreme Drought that expanded, including Harris, Chattahoochee, Muscogee, Madison, Oglethorpe, and Wilkes counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, Fannin County remained primarily under D2 (Severe Drought) conditions throughout January. Following the initial onset of D2 on December 30, 2025, the county experienced a brief period of marginal improvement before conditions degraded back to D2 levels by late January. By the end of the month, the county had recorded a total of four weeks at or above the D2 threshold, with drought conditions persisting into February 2026.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall during this period led to further worsening of drought conditions. In addition, recent hard freeze events have dried available fuels, resulting in an increasing wildfire risk throughout our area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lack of rainfall during January combined with multiple freeze events resulted in worsening drought conditions into Extreme (D3) drought by the end of January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed in areas along and near the Blue Ridge escarpment during the late evening of the 24th, beginning mainly as sleet. The sleet, occasionally mixed with snow continued into the early morning of the 25th, when it began to mix with and eventually change to freezing rain. The freezing rain was mostly light until a band of heavy freezing rain moved over the area late in the afternoon. Total sleet accumulations of 1 to 2 inches occurred, with around 0.5 inch ice accretion on top of the sleet, resulting in very hazardous driving conditions. Unusually cold temperatures following the event resulted in travel difficulties that lingered for several days.

A more active weather pattern in the second half of the month allowed for several rounds of beneficial, albeit non-drought-breaking, rainfall, particularly over the north and northwest portions of the state. While these systems helped stabilize soil moisture in some areas, deep-layer moisture deficits remained significant, and led to the redevelopment of D3 Extreme Drought over portions of the state.||Precipitation for January varied across north and central Georgia. Much of north Georgia and the Metro Atlanta area received between 3.0 and 5.0 inches of rain, which neared 60 to 100 percent of the monthly normal. In contrast, central Georgia continued to lag behind, with many areas receiving only 1.5 to 3.0 inches (approximately 40 to 60 percent of normal). Despite the uptick in surface moisture, the cumulative rainfall deficit since August 2025 remained between 8 and 14 inches for a large swath of the Piedmont. Consequently, while topsoil showed signs of recovery, subsoil moisture and groundwater levels remained low for mid-winter.||Agricultural impacts continued to be reported through the Condition Monitoring Observer Reports (CMOR). The winter wheat crop, already stunted by the dry fall months, showed uneven growth and poor tillering in the most affected central Georgia counties. Livestock producers remained in a critical state; although January rains provided some minor recharge to stock ponds, many remained at dangerously low levels. The reliance on supplemental hay feeding remained total across central Georgia, and the high cost of feed continued to drive reports of decreasing herd size as producers looked toward a potentially dry spring.||The U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM) reflected a month of slow expansion of drying conditions. At the start of January, roughly 15.4 percent of north and central Georgia was in D2 Severe Drought. By the end of the month, this figure had shifted slightly to 50.96 percent. Although there were some improvements over portions of the western and northern greater Metro Atlanta area, there were also pockets of D3 Extreme Drought that expanded, including Harris, Chattahoochee, Muscogee, Madison, Oglethorpe, and Wilkes counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, Schley County remained in D2 (Severe Drought) conditions throughout the month of January. Following the initial onset of D2 on November 18, 2025, the county sustained 11 consecutive weeks of D2 conditions as of the end of January. These conditions remained unchanged as the event persisted into February 2026.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough approached Georgia on the morning of Sunday, January 18th, drawing Gulf moisture northward into central Georgia. Meanwhile, a cold front brought an influx of colder air into the area. This interaction led to a few hours of snowfall across much of central Georgia on the morning of January 18th. Snowfall accumulations were generally in the 1-2 range across this stretch of the state, though a few spots received near 3 of snow. After a brief period of slowed travel and snowy scenes, afternoon sun brought warmer temperatures which quickly melted the snow, bringing a quick end to this relatively uncommon central Georgia snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain transitioned to snow in the early morning hours of the 18th. Snow accumulations reached T to 1.5 inches across the county by mid morning. Remnant snow showers continued through late morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex setup characterized by moisture overrunning an entrenched wedge of cold air at the surface led to freezing rain and sleet beginning across north Georgia late on the night of January 24 and persisting through much of the day on January 25. Widespread ice accretion of one-tenth to one-quarter inch ice occurred with some areas receiving over a half inch of freezing rain accumulation. ||Significant tree damage and power outages occurred in the worst-affected areas of northeast Georgia, while spotty tree damage and isolated power outages occurred in other areas. Additionally, a layer of sleet occurred in much of northeast Georgia, leading to hazardous roadways. ||The hardest hit areas locally were White County as well as portions of Dawson and Lumpkin counties. The vast majority of the White County lost power with many locations remaining without power for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light rain showers began on the evening of the 24th. Rain briefly transitioned to ice on the morning of the 25th, however temperatures warmed through the day and quickly melted any accumulations which managed to stick on elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall during this period led to further worsening of drought conditions. In addition, recent hard freeze events have dried available fuels, resulting in an increasing wildfire risk throughout our area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lack of rainfall during January combined with multiple freeze events resulted in worsening drought conditions into Extreme (D3) drought by the end of January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed in areas along and near the Blue Ridge escarpment during the late evening of the 24th, beginning mainly as sleet. The sleet, occasionally mixed with snow continued into the early morning of the 25th, when it began to mix with and eventually change to freezing rain. The freezing rain was mostly light until a band of heavy freezing rain moved over the area late in the afternoon. Total sleet accumulations of 1 to 2 inches occurred, with around 0.5 inch ice accretion on top of the sleet, resulting in very hazardous driving conditions. Unusually cold temperatures following the event resulted in travel difficulties that lingered for several days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously deep upper low barreled into the region on Saturday, January 31, bringing very cold temperatures. As the low dropped southeastward into the Carolinas, moisture wrapped around the backside of the low, bringing bands of snowfall across much of northeast and east-central Georgia. Snowfall totals were maximized where these bands occurred, reaching as high as 4-7 in many areas. ||Given the narrow nature of these snow bands, areas just a few miles to the west (including much of metro Atlanta) saw very little snowfall, though windy and very cold conditions occurred areawide. Wind chills reached dangerously cold subzero levels across much of the area by the night of January 31 into the morning of February 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow shower began on the morning of the 31s and continued off and on through the afternoon. Total snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 4 inches with the greatest amount to the north and east due to a narrow band of heavier snowfall in the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted much of southeast Georgia with significant snow accumulations and very cold wind chill temperatures. An area of low pressure shifted off the Southeast coast while deepening, ushering in a very cold air mass across the region. The depth of cold air was sufficient to support snow as the primary precipitation type. Rain quickly transitioned to snow during the afternoon hours, which continued into the evening before ending. Most of the snow accumulation impacted areas along and north of Interstate 16, with the highest amounts reported across Screven, Bulloch, and Effingham counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow was reported across the entire inland half of Chatham County. While most locations received total accumulations of less than an inch, one CoCoRaHS observer reported 3.2 inches of total accumulation near the Effingham County line near the intersection of Highway 30 and Hodgeville Road. The official daily snowfall total at the Savannah/Hilton Head International Airport (KSAV) was 0.5 inches. Chatham County Emergency Management also reported that 8 different bridges were iced over and closed, including multiple accidents at a few of these locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell across the coastal half of Chatham County. In most locations, snowfall amounts were no more than a trace or a dusting. However, there were a couple of reports of up to half of an inch of snow accumulation on Tybee Island. Also, a picture shared on social media showed that the Lazaretto Creek bridge was mostly covered in snow and ice as well as the entrance to Fort Pulaski off of Highway 80.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, Chattahoochee County experienced a significant degradation into D3 (Extreme Drought) during January. Following the initial onset of D2 (Severe Drought) on October 14, 2025, the county sustained 16 consecutive weeks of D2 or greater intensity. This streak includes the intensification to D3, which was officially recorded on January 20. The drought persisted into February 2026.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Severe to Extreme Drought that characterized Fall 2025 persisted into January 2026 as north and central Georgia failed to benefit from the typical 'recharge' period. A more active weather pattern in the second half of the month allowed for several rounds of beneficial, albeit non-drought-breaking, rainfall, particularly over the north and northwest portions of the state. While these systems helped stabilize soil moisture in some areas, deep-layer moisture deficits remained significant, and led to the redevelopment of D3 Extreme Drought over portions of the state.||Precipitation for January varied across north and central Georgia. Much of north Georgia and the Metro Atlanta area received between 3.0 and 5.0 inches of rain, which neared 60 to 100 percent of the monthly normal. In contrast, central Georgia continued to lag behind, with many areas receiving only 1.5 to 3.0 inches (approximately 40 to 60 percent of normal). Despite the uptick in surface moisture, the cumulative rainfall deficit since August 2025 remained between 8 and 14 inches for a large swath of the Piedmont. Consequently, while topsoil showed signs of recovery, subsoil moisture and groundwater levels remained low for mid-winter.||Agricultural impacts continued to be reported through the Condition Monitoring Observer Reports (CMOR). EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex setup characterized by moisture overrunning an entrenched wedge of cold air at the surface led to freezing rain and sleet beginning across north Georgia late on the night of January 24 and persisting through much of the day on January 25. Widespread ice accretion of one-tenth to one-quarter inch ice occurred with some areas receiving over a half inch of freezing rain accumulation. ||Significant tree damage and power outages occurred in the worst-affected areas of northeast Georgia, while spotty tree damage and isolated power outages occurred in other areas. Additionally, a layer of sleet occurred in much of northeast Georgia, leading to hazardous roadways. ||The hardest hit areas locally were White County as well as portions of Dawson and Lumpkin counties. The vast majority of the White County lost power with many locations remaining without power for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light rain showers began on the evening of the 24th. Rain briefly transitioned to ice on the morning of the 25th, however temperatures warmed through the day and quickly melted any accumulations which managed to stick on elevated surfaces. Northern portions of the county which were in the wedge were able to remain colder for a little longer and resulted in ice accumulations on elevated surface up to 0.15 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet showers began through the late evening and overnight hours of the 24th. Sleet accumulations transitioned over to freezing rain through the early morning of the 25th and continued through the day. The heaviest ice accumulations occurred with the second push of precipitation in the afternoon of the 25th. All precipitation ended Thursday evening with total ice accumulations of 0.25 to 0.5 across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously deep upper low barreled into the region on Saturday, January 31, bringing very cold temperatures. As the low dropped southeastward into the Carolinas, moisture wrapped around the backside of the low, bringing bands of snowfall across much of northeast and east-central Georgia. Snowfall totals were maximized where these bands occurred, reaching as high as 4-7 in many areas. ||Given the narrow nature of these snow bands, areas just a few miles to the west (including much of metro Atlanta) saw very little snowfall, though windy and very cold conditions occurred areawide. Wind chills reached dangerously cold subzero levels across much of the area by the night of January 31 into the morning of February 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on the morning of the 31st and continued into the afternoon. Total snowfall across the county was 1 to 2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted much of southeast Georgia with significant snow accumulations and very cold wind chill temperatures. An area of low pressure shifted off the Southeast coast while deepening, ushering in a very cold air mass across the region. The depth of cold air was sufficient to support snow as the primary precipitation type. Rain quickly transitioned to snow during the afternoon hours, which continued into the evening before ending. Most of the snow accumulation impacted areas along and north of Interstate 16, with the highest amounts reported across Screven, Bulloch, and Effingham counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on numerous reports, snowfall totals across Screven County generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches with locally higher amounts. The highest measured amounts included 4.5 inches in Sylvania, 3 to 3.5 inches around Hilltonia, 3 inches around Woodcliff, and 3 inches in Newington. Post event analysis shows that the lightest accumulations occurred in the western portion of the county while the heaviest occurred across the eastern portion of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall accumulations ranged from 1 to 3 inches across Bulloch County, highest across the eastern half of the county. The highest reported accumulations included 2.8 inches near Hopeulikit, 2.5 inches near Brooklet, 2.2 inches southeast of Statesboro and 1.9 inches near Dover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Severe to Extreme Drought that characterized Fall 2025 persisted into January 2026 as north and central Georgia failed to benefit from the typical 'recharge' period. A more active weather pattern in the second half of the month allowed for several rounds of beneficial, albeit non-drought-breaking, rainfall, particularly over the north and northwest portions of the state. While these systems helped stabilize soil moisture in some areas, deep-layer moisture deficits remained significant, and led to the redevelopment of D3 Extreme Drought over portions of the state.||Precipitation for January varied across north and central Georgia. Despite the uptick in surface moisture, the cumulative rainfall deficit since August 2025 remained between 8 and 14 inches for a large swath of the Piedmont. Consequently, while topsoil showed signs of recovery, subsoil moisture and groundwater levels remained low for mid-winter.||Agricultural impacts continued to be reported through the Condition Monitoring Observer Reports (CMOR). The winter wheat crop, already stunted by the dry fall months, showed uneven growth and poor tillering in the most affected central Georgia counties. Livestock producers remained in a critical state; although January rains provided some minor recharge to stock ponds, many remained at dangerously low levels. The reliance on supplemental hay feeding remained total across central Georgia, and the high cost of feed continued to drive reports of decreasing herd size as producers looked toward a potentially dry spring.||The U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM) reflected a month of slow expansion of drying conditions. At the start of January, roughly 15.4 percent of north and central Georgia was in D2 Severe Drought. By the end of the month, this figure had shifted slightly to 50.96 percent. Although there were some improvements over portions of the western and northern greater Metro Atlanta area, there were also pockets of D3 Extreme Drought that expanded, including Harris, Chattahoochee, Muscogee, Madison, Oglethorpe, and Wilkes counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, Dade County entered January under D2 (Severe Drought) conditions. Following a period of improvement, the county saw a reduction in drought intensity, officially falling below the D2 drought thresholds on January 12. This event, which originally reached the D2 threshold on December 30, 2025, peaked in both spatial extent and intensity on January 6. In total, the county sustained two weeks of D2 conditions during this brief event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough approached Georgia on the morning of Sunday, January 18th, drawing Gulf moisture northward into central Georgia. Meanwhile, a cold front brought an influx of colder air into the area. This interaction led to a few hours of snowfall across much of central Georgia on the morning of January 18th. Snowfall accumulations were generally in the 1-2 range across this stretch of the state, though a few spots received near 3 of snow. After a brief period of slowed travel and snowy scenes, afternoon sun brought warmer temperatures which quickly melted the snow, bringing a quick end to this relatively uncommon central Georgia snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain transitioned to snow in the early morning hours of the 18th. Snowfall accumulations of T to 2 inches were reported across the county by mid morning. All snow showers ended by late morning.

Snowfall totals were maximized where these bands occurred, reaching as high as 4-7 in many areas. ||Given the narrow nature of these snow bands, areas just a few miles to the west (including much of metro Atlanta) saw very little snowfall, though windy and very cold conditions occurred areawide. Wind chills reached dangerously cold subzero levels across much of the area by the night of January 31 into the morning of February 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began falling late in the morning on the 31st. Light snow showers continued through the afternoon with accumulations only reaching trace amounts across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Severe to Extreme Drought that characterized Fall 2025 persisted into January 2026 as north and central Georgia failed to benefit from the typical 'recharge' period. A more active weather pattern in the second half of the month allowed for several rounds of beneficial, albeit non-drought-breaking, rainfall, particularly over the north and northwest portions of the state. While these systems helped stabilize soil moisture in some areas, deep-layer moisture deficits remained significant, and led to the redevelopment of D3 Extreme Drought over portions of the state.||Precipitation for January varied across north and central Georgia. Much of north Georgia and the Metro Atlanta area received between 3.0 and 5.0 inches of rain, which neared 60 to 100 percent of the monthly normal. In contrast, central Georgia continued to lag behind, with many areas receiving only 1.5 to 3.0 inches (approximately 40 to 60 percent of normal). Despite the uptick in surface moisture, the cumulative rainfall deficit since August 2025 remained between 8 and 14 inches for a large swath of the Piedmont. Consequently, while topsoil showed signs of recovery, subsoil moisture and groundwater levels remained low for mid-winter.||Agricultural impacts continued to be reported through the Condition Monitoring Observer Reports (CMOR). The winter wheat crop, already stunted by the dry fall months, showed uneven growth and poor tillering in the most affected central Georgia counties. Livestock producers remained in a critical state; although January rains provided some minor recharge to stock ponds, many remained at dangerously low levels. The reliance on supplemental hay feeding remained total across central Georgia, and the high cost of feed continued to drive reports of decreasing herd size as producers looked toward a potentially dry spring.||The U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM) reflected a month of slow expansion of drying conditions. At the start of January, roughly 15.4 percent of north and central Georgia was in D2 Severe Drought. By the end of the month, this figure had shifted slightly to 50.96 percent. Although there were some improvements over portions of the western and northern greater Metro Atlanta area, there were also pockets of D3 Extreme Drought that expanded, including Harris, Chattahoochee, Muscogee, Madison, Oglethorpe, and Wilkes counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, D2 (Severe Drought) conditions returned to Johnson County on January 13. This degradation followed a brief mid-winter reprieve, which had interrupted an initial eight-week period of Severe Drought that began on October 21. By the end of January, the county had recorded a cumulative total of 11 weeks at or above the D2 threshold since the drought���s inception in the fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough approached Georgia on the morning of Sunday, January 18th, drawing Gulf moisture northward into central Georgia. Meanwhile, a cold front brought an influx of colder air into the area. This interaction led to a few hours of snowfall across much of central Georgia on the morning of January 18th. Snowfall accumulations were generally in the 1-2 range across this stretch of the state, though a few spots received near 3 of snow. After a brief period of slowed travel and snowy scenes, afternoon sun brought warmer temperatures which quickly melted the snow, bringing a quick end to this relatively uncommon central Georgia snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain transitioned to snow in the morning hours of the 18th and quickly accumulated 0.5 to 2 inches of snowfall by 9am. All remnant snow showers cleared the area by 10am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex setup characterized by moisture overrunning an entrenched wedge of cold air at the surface led to freezing rain and sleet beginning across north Georgia late on the night of January 24 and persisting through much of the day on January 25. Widespread ice accretion of one-tenth to one-quarter inch ice occurred with some areas receiving over a half inch of freezing rain accumulation. ||Significant tree damage and power outages occurred in the worst-affected areas of northeast Georgia, while spotty tree damage and isolated power outages occurred in other areas. Additionally, a layer of sleet occurred in much of northeast Georgia, leading to hazardous roadways. ||The hardest hit areas locally were White County as well as portions of Dawson and Lumpkin counties. The vast majority of the White County lost power with many locations remaining without power for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet showers began through the late evening and overnight hours of the 24th. Sleet accumulations transitioned over to freezing rain through the early morning of the 25th and continued through the day. The heaviest ice accumulations occurred with the second push of precipitation in the afternoon of the 25th. All precipitation ended Thursday evening with total ice accumulations of 0.25 to 0.5 across the county.

The reliance on supplemental hay feeding remained total across central Georgia, and the high cost of feed continued to drive reports of decreasing herd size as producers looked toward a potentially dry spring.||The U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM) reflected a month of slow expansion of drying conditions. At the start of January, roughly 15.4 percent of north and central Georgia was in D2 Severe Drought. By the end of the month, this figure had shifted slightly to 50.96 percent. Although there were some improvements over portions of the western and northern greater Metro Atlanta area, there were also pockets of D3 Extreme Drought that expanded, including Harris, Chattahoochee, Muscogee, Madison, Oglethorpe, and Wilkes counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, D2 (Severe Drought) conditions returned to Twiggs County on January 13. This degradation followed a brief mid-winter reprieve, which had interrupted an initial three-week period of Severe Drought that began on November 4. By the end of January, the county had recorded a cumulative total of six weeks at or above the D2 threshold since the drought���s inception in the fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Severe to Extreme Drought that characterized Fall 2025 persisted into January 2026 as north and central Georgia failed to benefit from the typical 'recharge' period. A more active weather pattern in the second half of the month allowed for several rounds of beneficial, albeit non-drought-breaking, rainfall, particularly over the north and northwest portions of the state. While these systems helped stabilize soil moisture in some areas, deep-layer moisture deficits remained significant, and led to the redevelopment of D3 Extreme Drought over portions of the state.||Precipitation for January varied across north and central Georgia. Much of north Georgia and the Metro Atlanta area received between 3.0 and 5.0 inches of rain, which neared 60 to 100 percent of the monthly normal. In contrast, central Georgia continued to lag behind, with many areas receiving only 1.5 to 3.0 inches (approximately 40 to 60 percent of normal). Despite the uptick in surface moisture, the cumulative rainfall deficit since August 2025 remained between 8 and 14 inches for a large swath of the Piedmont. Consequently, while topsoil showed signs of recovery, subsoil moisture and groundwater levels remained low for mid-winter.||Agricultural impacts continued to be reported through the Condition Monitoring Observer Reports (CMOR). EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex setup characterized by moisture overrunning an entrenched wedge of cold air at the surface led to freezing rain and sleet beginning across north Georgia late on the night of January 24 and persisting through much of the day on January 25. Widespread ice accretion of one-tenth to one-quarter inch ice occurred with some areas receiving over a half inch of freezing rain accumulation. ||Significant tree damage and power outages occurred in the worst-affected areas of northeast Georgia, while spotty tree damage and isolated power outages occurred in other areas. Additionally, a layer of sleet occurred in much of northeast Georgia, leading to hazardous roadways. ||The hardest hit areas locally were White County as well as portions of Dawson and Lumpkin counties. The vast majority of the White County lost power with many locations remaining without power for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light rain showers began through the late evening and overnight hours of the 24th. Rain accumulations transitioned over to freezing rain through the early morning of the 25th and continued through the day. The heaviest ice accumulations occurred with the second push of precipitation in the afternoon of the 25th. All precipitation ended Thursday evening with total ice accumulations of 0.1 to 0.25 across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously deep upper low barreled into the region on Saturday, January 31, bringing very cold temperatures. As the low dropped southeastward into the Carolinas, moisture wrapped around the backside of the low, bringing bands of snowfall across much of northeast and east-central Georgia. Snowfall totals were maximized where these bands occurred, reaching as high as 4-7 in many areas. ||Given the narrow nature of these snow bands, areas just a few miles to the west (including much of metro Atlanta) saw very little snowfall, though windy and very cold conditions occurred areawide. Wind chills reached dangerously cold subzero levels across much of the area by the night of January 31 into the morning of February 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall began early in the morning on the 31st with a moderate to heavy band setting up over the area from north to south. Accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were reported with the highest amounts in a narrow swath of only a few miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A unique snow setup for GA developed as a strong coastal low strengthening offshore, while a upper level disturbance shifted over our area. This provided sufficient synoptic and mesoscale lift for widespread heavy snow across eastern GA along with unusually high snow to liquid ratios between 15-20 to 1. The bulk of the persistent heavy snow remained in SC but a period of moderate-heavy snow occurred within banding along the western flank of the system. This band produced the majority of the accumulating snow for eastern GA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public and trained spotter measurements of 5.5 inches of snow around Grovetown. Additional reports of over 4 inches around Evans. 2.0 criteria met around 1400 local.

The Severe to Extreme Drought that characterized Fall 2025 persisted into January 2026 as north and central Georgia failed to benefit from the typical 'recharge' period. A more active weather pattern in the second half of the month allowed for several rounds of beneficial, albeit non-drought-breaking, rainfall, particularly over the north and northwest portions of the state. While these systems helped stabilize soil moisture in some areas, deep-layer moisture deficits remained significant, and led to the redevelopment of D3 Extreme Drought over portions of the state.||Precipitation for January varied across north and central Georgia. Much of north Georgia and the Metro Atlanta area received between 3.0 and 5.0 inches of rain, which neared 60 to 100 percent of the monthly normal. In contrast, central Georgia continued to lag behind, with many areas receiving only 1.5 to 3.0 inches (approximately 40 to 60 percent of normal). Despite the uptick in surface moisture, the cumulative rainfall deficit since August 2025 remained between 8 and 14 inches for a large swath of the Piedmont. Consequently, while topsoil showed signs of recovery, subsoil moisture and groundwater levels remained low for mid-winter.||Agricultural impacts continued to be reported through the Condition Monitoring Observer Reports (CMOR). The winter wheat crop, already stunted by the dry fall months, showed uneven growth and poor tillering in the most affected central Georgia counties. Livestock producers remained in a critical state; although January rains provided some minor recharge to stock ponds, many remained at dangerously low levels. The reliance on supplemental hay feeding remained total across central Georgia, and the high cost of feed continued to drive reports of decreasing herd size as producers looked toward a potentially dry spring.||The U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM) reflected a month of slow expansion of drying conditions. At the start of January, roughly 15.4 percent of north and central Georgia was in D2 Severe Drought. By the end of the month, this figure had shifted slightly to 50.96 percent. Although there were some improvements over portions of the western and northern greater Metro Atlanta area, there were also pockets of D3 Extreme Drought that expanded, including Harris, Chattahoochee, Muscogee, Madison, Oglethorpe, and Wilkes counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, D2 (Severe Drought) conditions returned to Wilcox County on January 13. This degradation followed a brief mid-winter reprieve, which had interrupted an initial five-week period of Severe Drought that began on November 11. By the end of January, the county had recorded a cumulative total of eight weeks at or above the D2 threshold since the drought���s inception in the fall.

By the end of January, the county had recorded a cumulative total of 7 weeks at the D2 threshold since the drought���s inception in the fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex setup characterized by moisture overrunning an entrenched wedge of cold air at the surface led to freezing rain and sleet beginning across north Georgia late on the night of January 24 and persisting through much of the day on January 25. Widespread ice accretion of one-tenth to one-quarter inch ice occurred with some areas receiving over a half inch of freezing rain accumulation. ||Significant tree damage and power outages occurred in the worst-affected areas of northeast Georgia, while spotty tree damage and isolated power outages occurred in other areas. Additionally, a layer of sleet occurred in much of northeast Georgia, leading to hazardous roadways. ||The hardest hit areas locally were White County as well as portions of Dawson and Lumpkin counties. The vast majority of the White County lost power with many locations remaining without power for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light rain showers began on the evening of the 24th. Surfaces and air temperatures remained just above freezing through the event and resulted in no ice accumulations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously deep upper low barreled into the region on Saturday, January 31, bringing very cold temperatures. As the low dropped southeastward into the Carolinas, moisture wrapped around the backside of the low, bringing bands of snowfall across much of northeast and east-central Georgia. Snowfall totals were maximized where these bands occurred, reaching as high as 4-7 in many areas. ||Given the narrow nature of these snow bands, areas just a few miles to the west (including much of metro Atlanta) saw very little snowfall, though windy and very cold conditions occurred areawide. Wind chills reached dangerously cold subzero levels across much of the area by the night of January 31 into the morning of February 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow flurries began in the morning hours with little to no accumulations. Accumulations sharply increased to the north and east where a narrow and heavy band of snow had set up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A unique snow setup for GA developed as a strong coastal low strengthening offshore, while a upper level disturbance shifted over our area. This provided sufficient synoptic and mesoscale lift for widespread heavy snow across eastern GA along with unusually high snow to liquid ratios between 15-20 to 1. The bulk of the persistent heavy snow remained in SC but a period of moderate-heavy snow occurred within banding along the western flank of the system. This band produced the majority of the accumulating snow for eastern GA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple public reports of 3 to 4 inches of snow accumulation around Waynesboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 drought conditions redeveloped during the middle of January and continued into February.

A strong upper trough approached Georgia on the morning of Sunday, January 18th, drawing Gulf moisture northward into central Georgia. Meanwhile, a cold front brought an influx of colder air into the area. This interaction led to a few hours of snowfall across much of central Georgia on the morning of January 18th. Snowfall accumulations were generally in the 1-2 range across this stretch of the state, though a few spots received near 3 of snow. After a brief period of slowed travel and snowy scenes, afternoon sun brought warmer temperatures which quickly melted the snow, bringing a quick end to this relatively uncommon central Georgia snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain transitioned to snowfall in the morning hours of the 18th, with 1 to 2 inches of accumulation across the county before clearing out by the late morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in a week, a winter storm impacted the mountains of northeast Georgia as an area of low pressure intensified across the Deep South. In this case\, all precipitation fell as snow. Snow began falling during the late evening of the 30th and continued off and on for around 24 hours\, with periods of heavy snow becoming common during the morning of the 31st. By the time the snow tapered off\, 5 to 8 inch totals along with locally higher amounts were reported across the area.

A strong upper trough approached Georgia on the morning of Sunday, January 18th, drawing Gulf moisture northward into central Georgia. Meanwhile, a cold front brought an influx of colder air into the area. This interaction led to a few hours of snowfall across much of central Georgia on the morning of January 18th. Snowfall accumulations were generally in the 1-2 range across this stretch of the state, though a few spots received near 3 of snow. After a brief period of slowed travel and snowy scenes, afternoon sun brought warmer temperatures which quickly melted the snow, bringing a quick end to this relatively uncommon central Georgia snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain transitioned to snow in the morning hours of the 18th. Snowfall accumulated to between T ant 1 inch across the county, tapering off by the late morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex setup characterized by moisture overrunning an entrenched wedge of cold air at the surface led to freezing rain and sleet beginning across north Georgia late on the night of January 24 and persisting through much of the day on January 25. Widespread ice accretion of one-tenth to one-quarter inch ice occurred with some areas receiving over a half inch of freezing rain accumulation. ||Significant tree damage and power outages occurred in the worst-affected areas of northeast Georgia, while spotty tree damage and isolated power outages occurred in other areas. Additionally, a layer of sleet occurred in much of northeast Georgia, leading to hazardous roadways. ||The hardest hit areas locally were White County as well as portions of Dawson and Lumpkin counties. The vast majority of the White County lost power with many locations remaining without power for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light rain showers began on the evening of the 24th. Surfaces and air temperatures remained just above freezing through the event and resulted in no ice accumulations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously deep upper low barreled into the region on Saturday, January 31, bringing very cold temperatures. As the low dropped southeastward into the Carolinas, moisture wrapped around the backside of the low, bringing bands of snowfall across much of northeast and east-central Georgia. Snowfall totals were maximized where these bands occurred, reaching as high as 4-7 in many areas. ||Given the narrow nature of these snow bands, areas just a few miles to the west (including much of metro Atlanta) saw very little snowfall, though windy and very cold conditions occurred areawide. Wind chills reached dangerously cold subzero levels across much of the area by the night of January 31 into the morning of February 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers began in the morning and continued off and on through the day. Total snow accumulations ranged from 0.5 to 2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in a week, a winter storm impacted the Piedmont and foothills of northeast Georgia as an area of low pressure intensified across the Deep South. In this case, the bulk of the precipitation fell as snow. Snow began falling during the pre-dawn hours of the 31st and continued through much of the day. By the time the snow tapered off during the evening, 4 to 7 inches with locally higher amounts were reported.

Widespread ice accretion of one-tenth to one-quarter inch ice occurred with some areas receiving over a half inch of freezing rain accumulation. ||Significant tree damage and power outages occurred in the worst-affected areas of northeast Georgia, while spotty tree damage and isolated power outages occurred in other areas. Additionally, a layer of sleet occurred in much of northeast Georgia, leading to hazardous roadways. ||The hardest hit areas locally were White County as well as portions of Dawson and Lumpkin counties. The vast majority of the White County lost power with many locations remaining without power for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light rain showers began on the evening of the 24th. Rain briefly transitioned to ice on the morning of the 25th, however temperatures warmed through the day and quickly melted any accumulations, less than 0.1 inches, which managed to stick on elevated surfaces.

All precipitation ended Thursday evening with total ice accumulations of 0.1 to 0.3 across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously deep upper low barreled into the region on Saturday, January 31, bringing very cold temperatures. As the low dropped southeastward into the Carolinas, moisture wrapped around the backside of the low, bringing bands of snowfall across much of northeast and east-central Georgia. Snowfall totals were maximized where these bands occurred, reaching as high as 4-7 in many areas. ||Given the narrow nature of these snow bands, areas just a few miles to the west (including much of metro Atlanta) saw very little snowfall, though windy and very cold conditions occurred areawide. Wind chills reached dangerously cold subzero levels across much of the area by the night of January 31 into the morning of February 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers developed late on the 30th with a heavy band of snow developing through the morning of the 30th. This persistent band of heavier snowfall resulted in the highest observed accumulations for the area. Total accumulations for the county ranged form 4 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A unique snow setup for GA developed as a strong coastal low strengthening offshore, while a upper level disturbance shifted over our area. This provided sufficient synoptic and mesoscale lift for widespread heavy snow across eastern GA along with unusually high snow to liquid ratios between 15-20 to 1. The bulk of the persistent heavy snow remained in SC but a period of moderate-heavy snow occurred within banding along the western flank of the system. This band produced the majority of the accumulating snow for eastern GA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotted measured 4.5 inches of snow in Lincolnton GA as of 1930 local. Spotter measured over 2.0 at 1400 local.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in a week, a winter storm impacted the Piedmont of northeast Georgia as an area of low pressure intensified across the Deep South. In this case, after a brief period of rain, or a mix of rain and snow at the outset, the bulk of the precipitation fell as snow. Snow began falling during the pre-dawn hours of the 31st and continued through much of the day. By the time the snow tapered off during the afternoon, 3 to 5 inch totals were reported across much of the area.

A classic ice storm developed to the north and east of the CSRA on January 25th with eastern GA seeing modest impacts. Cold air damming at the surface and strong overrunning aloft produced light freezing rain across eastern GA. Hazardous road conditions were the primary impact with some minor tree and powerline damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Augusta Regional Airport (AGS) reported 0.07 accumulated freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed across the northeast Georgia Piedmont during the early morning of the 25th. The precipitation began as mostly sleet before mixing with, and eventually changing to freezing rain later in the morning. The precipitation finally tapered off during the evening. Total sleet accumulations of 0.25 to 0.5 inches occurred, with around 0.25 inch of ice accreting on top of the sleet. This made for very hazardous driving conditions. Due to unusually cold weather following the event, a couple of days of refreezing resulted in travel difficulties continuing during the overnight and morning hours.

A QLCS moved into western Georgia during the mid morning hours of the 10th. The strongest storms were located south of I20 with several bowing and comma-head sections which lead to pockets of strong wind damage and a brief tornado in Carrol county. Storms quickly weakened as they moved eastward, as a wedge was in place over northern Georgia.||Storms in north Georgia became nearly stationary ahead of the main line with high proficient rainfall rates along and south of the southern Appalachians. This resulted in areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on a vehicle on the 200 block of wilcox rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down just south of Lowell in southern|Carroll County late Saturday morning. The tornado was embedded |within a Quasi-Linear Convective System. Damage was first seen at|a residence near Lake Circle and Staples Dairy Road. A 2-story |home had a good portion of its roof cover pulled up along with the|southwest-facing wall/siding severely damaged. The structure |remained intact. Adjacent to the home, a barn was nearly destroyed|with several 6' x 6' poles ripped out of the ground (secured in |the ground by concrete). Based on this damage, it is estimated the|tornado reached near its peak intensity of 90- 95 mph (EF-1). It |was in this area, a CC drop/TDS on FFC radar occurred. Debris from|the barn was thrown more than 300 yards away. The tornado |continued to track east damaging another barn and uprooting |several trees as it moved across Liberty Church Road. Another |older barn at a residence just east of Liberty Church Road |sustained significant damage, with most of its (sheet-metal) roof|ripped off and walls destroyed. Additional trees were seen |snapped or uprooted at this location and about 1/4 mile downstream|in a wooded area (seen also by drone footage). Beyond this final |point, no notable damage was seen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Severe to Extreme Drought that characterized Fall 2025 persisted into January 2026 as north and central Georgia failed to benefit from the typical 'recharge' period. A more active weather pattern in the second half of the month allowed for several rounds of beneficial, albeit non-drought-breaking, rainfall, particularly over the north and northwest portions of the state. While these systems helped stabilize soil moisture in some areas, deep-layer moisture deficits remained significant, and led to the redevelopment of D3 Extreme Drought over portions of the state.||Precipitation for January varied across north and central Georgia. Much of north Georgia and the Metro Atlanta area received between 3.0 and 5.0 inches of rain, which neared 60 to 100 percent of the monthly normal. In contrast, central Georgia continued to lag behind, with many areas receiving only 1.5 to 3.0 inches (approximately 40 to 60 percent of normal). Despite the uptick in surface moisture, the cumulative rainfall deficit since August 2025 remained between 8 and 14 inches for a large swath of the Piedmont. Consequently, while topsoil showed signs of recovery, subsoil moisture and groundwater levels remained low for mid-winter.||Agricultural impacts continued to be reported through the Condition Monitoring Observer Reports (CMOR). The winter wheat crop, already stunted by the dry fall months, showed uneven growth and poor tillering in the most affected central Georgia counties. Livestock producers remained in a critical state; although January rains provided some minor recharge to stock ponds, many remained at dangerously low levels. The reliance on supplemental hay feeding remained total across central Georgia, and the high cost of feed continued to drive reports of decreasing herd size as producers looked toward a potentially dry spring.||The U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM) reflected a month of slow expansion of drying conditions. At the start of January, roughly 15.4 percent of north and central Georgia was in D2 Severe Drought. By the end of the month, this figure had shifted slightly to 50.96 percent. Although there were some improvements over portions of the western and northern greater Metro Atlanta area, there were also pockets of D3 Extreme Drought that expanded, including Harris, Chattahoochee, Muscogee, Madison, Oglethorpe, and Wilkes counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, D2 (Severe Drought) conditions were introduced to Banks County on January 6. In total, the county sustained four weeks of D2 conditions, with drought continuing into February.

Following the initial onset of D2 on December 30, 2025, the county experienced a brief period of marginal improvement before conditions degraded back to D2 levels by late January. By the end of the month, the county had recorded a total of three weeks at or above the D2 threshold, with drought conditions persisting into February 2026.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex setup characterized by moisture overrunning an entrenched wedge of cold air at the surface led to freezing rain and sleet beginning across north Georgia late on the night of January 24 and persisting through much of the day on January 25. Widespread ice accretion of one-tenth to one-quarter inch ice occurred with some areas receiving over a half inch of freezing rain accumulation. ||Significant tree damage and power outages occurred in the worst-affected areas of northeast Georgia, while spotty tree damage and isolated power outages occurred in other areas. Additionally, a layer of sleet occurred in much of northeast Georgia, leading to hazardous roadways. ||The hardest hit areas locally were White County as well as portions of Dawson and Lumpkin counties. The vast majority of the White County lost power with many locations remaining without power for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light rain showers began on the evening of the 24th. Rain briefly transitioned to ice on the morning of the 25th, however temperatures warmed through the day and quickly melted any accumulations, less than 0.1 inches, which managed to stick on elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously deep upper low barreled into the region on Saturday, January 31, bringing very cold temperatures. As the low dropped southeastward into the Carolinas, moisture wrapped around the backside of the low, bringing bands of snowfall across much of northeast and east-central Georgia. Snowfall totals were maximized where these bands occurred, reaching as high as 4-7 in many areas. ||Given the narrow nature of these snow bands, areas just a few miles to the west (including much of metro Atlanta) saw very little snowfall, though windy and very cold conditions occurred areawide. Wind chills reached dangerously cold subzero levels across much of the area by the night of January 31 into the morning of February 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers began overnight on the 29th and continued through the afternoon the following day. A band of heavy snow set up through the morning resulting in a concentrated band of highest accumulations and a sharp gradient cut-off to the west. Total snow accumulations ranged from just 1 inch to 5 inches.
